subject: math
topic_title: Venn Diagrams and Problem Solving with Sets
source_title: New General Mathematics SSS 1 by M.F. Macrae et al
learning_objectives: Represent sets and their relationships using Venn diagrams
Solve problems involving two sets using Venn diagrams
Solve problems involving three sets using Venn diagrams
Interpret shaded regions in Venn diagrams

sections.heading: Venn Diagram Representations
sections.content: A Venn diagram uses a rectangle for the universal set and ovals (circles) for subsets. Key representations: the oval itself = subset A; shaded A' = complement (elements not in A); shaded overlap = A ∩ B; shaded combined = A ∪ B; non-overlapping ovals = disjoint sets (P ∩ Q = ∅); one oval inside another = P ⊂ Q; shaded outside both = P' ∩ Q'; for three sets, the central overlap = P ∩ Q ∩ R.

sections.heading: Solving Problems with Two Sets
sections.content: For two-set problems, let x be the unknown (e.g., elements in both sets). Form an equation: (elements in A only) + (elements in both) + (elements in B only) + (elements in neither) = total. Example: 400 students; 300 take Biology; 190 take Chemistry; 70 take neither. Let x = n(B ∩ C). Then (300 − x) + x + (190 − x) + 70 = 400 → 560 − x = 400 → x = 160. So 160 take both, and 330 take at least one.

sections.heading: Solving Problems with Three Sets
sections.content: For three-set problems, let x = number in all three sets. Work outward from the centre: find expressions for each region in terms of x, then sum all regions = total. Example: 290 readers; 181 read Daily Times (D), 142 read Guardian (G), 117 read Punch (P); n(D∩G)=75, n(D∩P)=60, n(G∩P)=54. Summing all regions: 251 + x = 290 → x = 39. Then: reading exactly two papers = 189 − 3(39) = 72; exactly one = 62 + 3(39) = 179.
